segments.purgatorio-22-g60: Ed is the conjunction 'and' used to link this speech to what came before. 'Elli' is an archaic/poetic form for 'he' (egli) functioning as the subject; 'a' is the preposition 'to'; 'lui' is the indirect-object pronoun 'him'. The whole phrase is an introductory formula meaning 'and he (said) to him:'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g61: Tu prima m’invïasti combines the subject pronoun 'tu' (you) with the temporal adverb 'prima' (first/before) and the verb invïasti (passato remoto 2nd person singular of inviare/send) with the enclitic object 'm'' = 'mi' (me). The diaeresis on ï signals vowel separation in the meter. 'verso Parnaso' gives the direction/toward Parnassus; 'a ber' is an infinitive of purpose 'to drink'; 'ne le sue grotte' uses the particle 'ne' (here locative/partitive 'in/of them') + the article 'le' and possessive 'sue' to mean 'in its caves.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g62: e prima appresso Dio m’alluminasti: 'e' = 'and'; 'prima' again marks sequence ('first' or 'before that'); 'appresso' here means 'after/next' (an adverb modifying the sequence); 'Dio' names God (object of orientation); 'm'' = enclitic 'mi' (me); alluminasti is passato remoto 2nd person singular of alluminare 'you enlightened/illuminated'. Together it says that, after the previous act, the addressee enlightened the speaker toward God.
segments.purgatorio-22-g63: Facesti is passato remoto 2nd person singular of fare: 'you made/did' and with come introduces a simile 'you did like...'. Quei is an archaic demonstrative/relative ('that one' / 'he who') referring back to the generic person; che begins the relative clause 'who/that'. 'Va di notte' is present indicative 'goes by night.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g64: This relative clause describes that person: 'che' = 'who/that'; 'porta' is present indicative 'carries'; 'il lume' 'the light'; 'dietro' 'behind' (showing position); 'e' 'and'; 'sé' is the reflexive pronoun 'himself' (emphatic/reflexive); 'non giova' is a present-tense negated predicate 'it does not help/benefit' — literally 'it is of no use to him.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g65: ma 'but'; 'dopo sé' literally 'after himself' (i.e. following himself); 'fa' is present indicative 'makes/causes'; 'le persone dotte' is the direct object 'the learned/educated people' — the idea: though the lampbearer does not benefit, he causes others (following him) to be learned. 'Fa' here means 'renders' or 'produces the effect that...'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g66: quando introduces the temporal clause 'when'; dicesti is passato remoto 2nd person singular of dire 'you said'. It prepares the direct quotation that follows.
segments.purgatorio-22-g67: Quoted line: 'Secol si rinova' — 'Secol' = 'age/era' (secol/ secolo) is subject; 'si' is the reflexive/impersonal clitic used to make the verb reflexive/impersonal; 'rinova' is present indicative 'renews.' Literally 'The age renews itself.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g68: This continuation uses present indicative verbs to announce changes: 'torna' = 'returns/comes back'; 'giustizia' = 'justice'; 'e' = 'and'; 'primo tempo umano' is a noun phrase 'the first/prime human time' (i.e. the original age of mankind). The order is poetic but the grammar is standard: verb + nouns describing what returns.
segments.purgatorio-22-g69: Final clause of the quotation: 'e progenïe scende da ciel nova' — 'e' = 'and'; 'progenïe' = 'progeny/offspring' (subject); 'scende' present indicative 'descends'; 'da ciel' = 'from heaven'; 'nova' = 'new' (postpositive adjective modifying progeny). The inversion places the adjective after the noun for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.purgatorio-22-g70: Per te poeta fui: 'Per te' means 'through/for you' (causal), placed before the predicate for emphasis. The subject 'io' is omitted and 'fui' is the past tense (passato remoto/past historic) of essere, giving 'I was'. 'Poeta' is a predicative noun linked to 'fui' (I was a poet).
segments.purgatorio-22-g71: per te cristiano: This repeats 'per te' for emphasis; the verb 'fui' is omitted by ellipsis and understood from the previous clause, so 'cristiano' is another predicative noun meaning 'a Christian.' The colon marks the close of this paired, elliptical statement.
segments.purgatorio-22-g72: 'ma perché veggi mei ciò ch’io disegno,' — here 'ma' = 'but'; 'perché' introduces a purpose clause ('so that'); 'veggi' is an archaic present subjunctive form (addressing 'you', i.e. 'that you may see'); 'mei' is an archaic/dialectal form meaning 'better' (meglio); 'ciò' = 'that', and 'ch’io disegno' = 'which I intend/plan' with 'disegno' present indicative meaning 'I intend.' The relative 'ch’' links 'ciò' to the subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-22-g73: 'a colorare stenderò la mano.' — 'a' + infinitive expresses purpose ('in order to'); 'colorare' is literal 'to color' used metaphorically as 'to embellish/put color on' (i.e. to add descriptive detail); 'stenderò' is future indicative 'I will stretch/extend'; 'la mano' is the direct object: literally 'the hand' (idiomatically, 'I will put forth my hand' = I will add my touch).
segments.purgatorio-22-g74: 'Già era ’l mondo tutto quanto pregno de la vera credenza,' — 'Già era' (imperfect) situates a prior state ('already was'); word order places 'l mondo' (the world) before the predicate; 'tutto quanto' is an intensifier meaning 'entirely/altogether'; 'pregno de' = 'pregnant with/full of' followed by 'la vera credenza' = 'the true belief/faith.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g75: 'seminata per li messaggi de l’etterno regno;' — 'seminata' is a past participle ('sown') that modifies 'credenza' (passive/participial phrase). 'per' here means 'by/through' (agency or means), and 'li messaggi' refers to 'messengers' or 'missions' sent from 'l'etterno regno' ('the eternal kingdom').
segments.purgatorio-22-g76: 'e la parola tua sopra toccata si consonava a’ nuovi predicanti;' — 'e' = 'and'; 'la parola tua' = 'your word'; 'sopra toccata' is a participial phrase 'touched upon above/mentioned earlier' (sopra + past participle); 'si consonava' is imperfect reflexive/impersonal 'it was in harmony' or 'it harmonized'; 'a’ nuovi predicanti' = 'with the new preachers' (predicanti used as noun).
segments.purgatorio-22-g77: 'ond’ io a visitarli presi usata.' — 'ond’' (onde/ondè) = 'therefore/whereupon'; 'io' is subject; 'a visitarli' is an infinitive phrase 'to visit them' functioning as the activity taken up; 'presi' (passato remoto) 'I took' and 'usata' (literally 'used') forms the idiom 'presi usata' = 'I adopted the custom' or 'I became accustomed' (i.e. I began to visit them regularly).
segments.purgatorio-22-g33: 'E se non fosse ch’io drizzai mia cura,' is a conditional protasis introduced by 'E se non fosse che...' (an archaic/poetic 'if it had not been that...'). 'fosse' is the imperfect subjunctive of essere used in this conditional clause; 'ch’' = che introduces the subordinate clause; 'drizzai' is passato remoto (1st sing.) of drizzare meaning 'I turned/raised/fixed' and takes 'mia cura' (my attention/care) as its object.
segments.purgatorio-22-g34: 'quand’ io intesi là dove tu chiame,' — 'quand’' is an abbreviated 'quando' (when); 'intesi' is passato remoto of intendere used here as 'I heard' (a common Dantean usage). 'là dove tu chiame' is a locative relative clause 'there where you call/signal'; 'chiame' is an archaic 2nd-person present form (poetic) of chiamare, here 'you call/shout'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g35: 'crucciato quasi a l’umana natura:' — 'crucciato' is a past participle meaning 'distressed/angry/upset' describing the speaker's feeling; 'quasi' = 'as if' or 'almost'; 'a l’' (to the) is the preposition + article (poetic spacing) giving 'a l’umana natura' = 'at human nature' (i.e. annoyed with or regarding human nature).
segments.purgatorio-22-g36: '‘Per che non reggi tu,' — here 'Per che' is an archaic form equivalent to modern 'perché' = 'why'; word order places the verb before the subject ('non reggi tu') for emphasis. 'reggi' is from reggere (here 'restrain/hold back'), 2nd person singular present; the clause is a direct address (vocative/question).
segments.purgatorio-22-g37: 'o sacra fame de l’oro,' — 'o' is the vocative particle 'O' (addressing something), 'sacra' is an adjective 'sacred/solemn', 'fame' means 'hunger/craving' here as a moralized personified appetite, and 'de l’oro' = 'for/of gold' (poetic spacing of 'del oro').
segments.purgatorio-22-g38: 'l’appetito de’ mortali?' — 'l’appetito' = 'the appetite/desire' and 'de’ mortali' = 'of mortals/men'; the phrase is the object of the preceding question ('Why do you not restrain ... the appetite of mortals?'). The contraction 'de’' is poetic for 'dei' or 'di i'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g39: This segment is the closing mark of the quoted speech (a closing quotation mark / comma in the original); it carries no lexical content to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-22-g40: 'voltando sentirei le giostre grame.' — 'voltando' is a gerund of voltare ('turning' or 'as I turned'); 'sentirei' is the first person conditional ('I would hear' / 'I should hear'), matching the protasis begun earlier ('E se non fosse...'). 'le giostre grame' = 'the doleful/sad jousts/contests' (giostre = contests/struggles, grame = wretched/doleful), with adjective agreeing in gender and number.
segments.purgatorio-22-g41: 'Allor m’accorsi che troppo aprir l’ali potean le mani a spendere,' — 'Allor' = 'then'; 'm’accorsi' = reflexive passato remoto 'I perceived/realized' (mi + accorsi). 'che' introduces the content clause. The clause that follows has a poetic word order: the infinitive phrase 'troppo aprir l’ali' ('to open the wings too much') is the action that 'le mani' (the hands) 'potean' (imperfect plural of potere, 'could') do 'a spendere' (for spending). Grammatically, 'potean' agrees with 'le mani' (subject placed after the verb for emphasis).
segments.purgatorio-22-g42: 'e pente’mi così di quel come de li altri mali.' — 'e' = 'and'; 'pente’mi' is an archaic/contracted reflexive verbal form meaning 'I repented' (verb + enclitic 'mi'); 'così' = 'thus'; 'di quel come de li altri mali' = 'of that as (I did) of the other evils' — a comparative construction equating his repentance for this sin with his repentance for other evils. 'li' is the masculine plural article before 'altri mali'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g43: 'Quanti risurgeran coi crini scemi per ignoranza,' — 'Quanti' = 'how many' (a plural interrogative/relative quantifier); 'risurgeran' is a future form (poetic) 'they will rise again' (risurgeranno); 'coi' = 'con i' (with the); 'crini scemi' = 'shorn/cropped hair' (an epithet describing penitents; 'scemi' here is adjectival and archaic in sense 'shorn' or 'diminished' rather than the modern 'foolish'); 'per ignoranza' = 'through ignorance'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g44: 'che di questa pecca toglie ’l penter vivendo e ne li stremi!' — 'che' = 'who/which'; 'di questa pecca' = 'from this sin'; 'toglie' (present) = 'takes away/removes' (i.e. deprives of); '’l penter' = 'the repentance' (penter = pentimento/repentance); 'vivendo' = gerund 'while living'; 'e ne li stremi' = 'and even in the last moments' ('ne' = 'in/of it, here 'in', 'li stremi' = 'the extremes/last moments' — an idiomatic Dantean phrase for death's end). The sense: this sin robs people of repentance both in life and at the very end.
segments.purgatorio-22-g151: This whole clause is a nominal sentence with a relative clause: 'Mele e locuste' is a coordinated plural subject ('apples and locusts'). 'furon' is an archaic/poetic form of the passato remoto third-person plural (modern furono) meaning 'were'. 'le vivande' is the predicate noun phrase (the foods) determined by the feminine plural article. The relative clause 'che nodriro il Batista nel diserto' modifies 'le vivande': 'che' is the relative pronoun 'which/that'; 'nodriro' is an archaic/polite spelling of the passato remoto third-person plural (modern nodrirono) agreeing with 'vivande' and meaning 'nourished/served as food for'; 'il Batista' is the direct object of 'nodriro'; 'nel diserto' is a locative prepositional phrase ('in the desert').
segments.purgatorio-22-g152: 'per ch’' is a compressed form of 'perché' meaning 'for which / therefore / because'; it introduces the reason or consequence. 'elli' here is an older/dialectal form used where modern Italian has 'egli' (he) and refers back to the Baptist. 'è' is present indicative 'is', giving a present-state predicate. 'glorïoso e tanto grande' are predicate adjectives joined by 'e', with 'tanto' functioning as a degree adverb modifying 'grande' ('so/so much great'). 'quanto' introduces a comparative clause 'as much as'; within that clause 'per lo Vangelio' ('by/through the Gospel') gives the source, and 'v’è aperto' is an impersonal/inverted locative construction: 'v’' (= vi) = 'in it' or 'there', 'è' = 'is', and 'aperto' is the past participle used adjectivally ('opened, revealed') — together meaning 'as is opened/revealed in the Gospel.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g103: Euripide is the name subject. v’ is a contraction of vi meaning 'there' (locative), and è is the third-person singular present of essere ('is') used predicatively. Nosco here is an archaic/dialectal form indicating the company ('with us' / 'among us') rather than the usual verb 'to know'; e introduces the next name (Antifonte). Together the clause says the person named is present among us.
segments.purgatorio-22-g104: A single proper name (Simonide) presented in a list; no inflectional change here—it's nominative in effect as part of the enumerated subjects.
segments.purgatorio-22-g105: Agatone is another name in the list; e links it to 'altri' ('and other'). Piùe is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'more' or 'many more'. 'Greci' is the noun governed by the relative clause introduced by che. Già here means 'formerly' or 'once'; di + lauro expresses the material/ornament ('with laurel') and ornar is an infinitive 'to adorn', while la fronte ('the brow') is the part ornamented. The relative clause (che già di lauro ornar la fronte) modifies 'Greci'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g106: Quivi is a locative adverb 'there'. Si is the reflexive/impersonal particle used with the verb to form a passive/impersonal construction; veggion is the archaic third-person-plural present of veggĕre/vedere ('are seen' / 'one sees'). De le genti tue ('of/among your people') is a prepositional phrase that identifies whose people; Antigone follows as one of the persons seen.
segments.purgatorio-22-g107: A simple coordination of two proper names: Deïfile and Argia, linked by e ('and'), continuing the enumeration of persons seen.
segments.purgatorio-22-g108: e ('and') connects Ismene to the previous list. Sì is an intensifier here meaning 'so' or 'so very' and trista is an adjective 'sad'; come fue uses a comparative-like construction 'as she was', with fue the passato remoto (archaic spelling) of essere meaning 'was'. The clause says Ismene is as sad as she formerly was.
segments.purgatorio-22-g109: Védeisi is the impersonal/passive form (si + vede) meaning 'one sees' or 'is seen' with poetic accent. Quella is a demonstrative 'that one' or 'that woman' and che introduces a relative clause: mostrò is passato remoto 'showed' or 'displayed' and Langia is a substantive (personified 'longing'/'lament'). The whole clause means 'one sees that woman who displayed Langia.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g110: Èvvi is an impersonal existential form ('there is' / 'there are'). La figlia di Tiresia is a noun phrase: la figlia ('the daughter') governed by di Tiresia ('of Tiresias'), identifying another person present.
segments.purgatorio-22-g111: Simple addition of another proper name: e ('and') Teti (Thetis), continuing the list of figures seen.
segments.purgatorio-22-g112: e ('and') con ('with') introduces accompaniment: le suore sue is 'her sisters' (suore here used in an archaic/literary sense 'sisters' rather than nuns), with sue agreeing in gender/number as the possessive 'her'. Deïdamia names the person who is with those sisters. The phrase means 'and Deïdamia with her sisters.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g113: 'Tacevansi' is an archaic pronominal/impersonal form meaning 'they were silent' (modern 'tacevano'/'stavano zitti'); the verb precedes the noun for emphasis, and 'ambedue già li poeti' simply marks 'both poets already'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g114: 'di novo' = 'anew' or 'once more'; 'attenti a riguardar' uses the adjective 'attenti' followed by the preposition 'a' + infinitive 'riguardar' ('to look at/around'), and 'dintorno' complements it with 'around.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g115: 'liberi da' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'free from'; 'saliri' is an archaic infinitive form (modern 'salire') used substantively here ('ascents' / 'having to climb'), and 'pareti' refers to rock faces or walls — both governed by 'da'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g116: Syntactically Dante uses an auxiliary + participle sequence: 'eran' (imperfect of 'essere') combined with 'rimase' functions like a compound past meaning 'had remained' (they remained behind). 'le quattro ancelle del giorno' = 'the four handmaids of the day' (a personified phrase).
segments.purgatorio-22-g117: 'la quinta era al temo' — 'era' is imperfect 'was'; 'al' = 'a + il' meaning 'at the'; 'temo' is an archaic/poetic locution here (a point/edge/verge or similar), so 'the fifth was at the verge.' The phrase sets up the participial clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-22-g118: 'drizzando' is the present participle (gerund) of 'drizzare' meaning 'raising/straightening/directing' and introduces a simultaneous action ('while/and raising the burning horn'). 'pur' = 'still/even', 'in sù' = 'upward', and 'l'ardente corno' is the object 'the burning horn' (a signal horn).
segments.purgatorio-22-g119: 'quando' ('when') introduces the moment at which the following speech occurs; 'il mio duca' ('my guide') is the subject who speaks next.
segments.purgatorio-22-g120: 'Io credo' = 'I believe / I think'; 'ch’' is the conjunction 'che' ('that'). 'a lo stremo' = 'at the extreme/edge' (adverbial phrase). 'le destre spalle' = 'the right shoulders' (direct object), 'volger' is the infinitive 'to turn', and 'ne' is a clitic particle referring back ('of it/them' / 'away from it'); 'convegna' is impersonal present 'it is fitting/appropriate' (3rd-person singular). Together: 'I think that at the extremity it is fitting to turn our right shoulders.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g121: 'girando' (gerund) gives the manner: 'by turning/circling the mountain.' 'come far solemo' is a compressed archaic expression meaning 'as we used to do' ('far' = infinitive 'to do/make'; 'solemo' is an archaic form from 'solere' meaning 'we were accustomed').
segments.purgatorio-22-g25: This clause is structured with the demonstrative subject Queste parole (these words) followed by the person affected Stazio and then a causative-like verb phrase: mover (infinitive) plus fenno, an archaic past plural meaning 'they made/caused' (modern fecero). Un poco a riso is an adverbial phrase 'a little to a smile' (i.e. into a slight smile), and pria is an adverb meaning 'before/at first'. The infinitive mover is governed by fenno ('[these words] caused [Statius] to move/SMILE').
segments.purgatorio-22-g26: Poscia is an adverb 'afterwards/then'. Rispuose is the 3rd-person singular past tense (preterite) of rispondere, 'answered'—so the line links the previous small laugh to his subsequent reply.
segments.purgatorio-22-g27: Ogne (archaic form of ogni) modifies tuo dir: here dir functions as a noun meaning 'saying/word'. D’amor is a genitive/partitive 'of love'. M’è is the clitic mi + è (is to me), so the predicate m’è caro cenno literally 'is to me a dear sign' (caro = dear, cenno = sign/token). Word order places the possessed noun phrase before the predicate 'is dear to me'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g28: Veramente is an adverb 'truly/indeed', modified by più volte 'more often/many times'. Appaion is an archaic 3rd-person plural present (modern appaiono) 'they appear/come to sight'. Che introduces a relative clause: danno a dubitar uses the construction dare a + infinitive meaning 'give cause to [do X]' (here 'cause one to doubt'). Falsa matera is the predicate 'a false matter/subject', and the final prepositional phrase per le vere ragion che son nascose gives the reason: 'because of the true reasons which are hidden' (son = sono, nascose = hidden).
segments.purgatorio-22-g29: La tua dimanda is a noun phrase ('your question') introduced by the feminine article + possessive. Tuo creder uses the infinitive creder as an abstract noun meaning 'your belief' (infinitive-as-noun, common in older Italian). The clitic m' (mi) is attached to the verb avvera (m'avvera = 'it confirms to me'); avvera takes an infinitival complement (avvera esser ...) meaning 'confirms that ...'. Ch' is the contracted conjunction che, and fossi is the imperfect subjunctive of essere, used in the subordinate clause to report the speaker's past state ('that I was ...').
segments.purgatorio-22-g30: Forse introduces a tentative adverb ('perhaps'). Per quella cerchia is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of/for that circle' (cerchia = 'circle' or 'tier', here a circle/level); dov' is the poetic contraction of dove (in cui) meaning 'in which', and era is the imperfect indicative 'I was' (era io = 'I was') — the relative clause modifies cerchia.
segments.purgatorio-22-g31: Or sappi is an exhortation ('now, know' — sappi is the imperative of sapere addressed to the listener). Ch' introduces the content clause: avarizia fu partita tropp[o] da me. Here fu + partita is a perfect/passive or resultative construction ('was parted/was removed'); troppo modifies the degree ('too much/too far'), and da me indicates source/agent ('from me' or 'in my case').
segments.purgatorio-22-g32: E this dismisura is the topicalized direct object ('and this excess') placed before the verb. Migliaia di lunari is the subject ('thousands of lunar [months/moons]'). Hanno punita uses avere (hanno) with the past participle punita; because the feminine direct object questa dismisura precedes the verb, the participle shows agreement (punita) — a poetic/archaic feature where the participle agrees with a preceding object.
segments.purgatorio-22-g45: E sappie is an archaic hortatory/clausal verb form meaning “know” or “let it be known” (Dante often uses a subjunctive/hortatory form here). The relative clause che rimbecca modifies la colpa: rimbecca is used in a literal sense of reproach/rebuke and takes per + noun to indicate the means ('by direct opposition') that checks or answers the sin (alcun peccato = 'any sin'). Word order is standard for a relative clause: antecedent (la colpa) + che + verb + complement.
segments.purgatorio-22-g46: Con esso insieme qui: con + pronoun esso ('with it' — the previously named fault/sin) + insieme ('together') + qui ('here/at this place in the purgatorial terrace'). Suo verde is a possessive + noun construction meaning 'its greenness' or 'its fresh green (foliage/green state)'; secca is 3rd-singular present (or poetically used) of seccare meaning 'to wither/dry up.' The verb agrees with the grammatical subject expressed by esso.
segments.purgatorio-22-g47: Però is a discourse adverb meaning 'therefore' (here giving the conclusion from the previous clause).
segments.purgatorio-22-g48: S’io son tra quella gente stato compresses the conditional protasis. S’io is the contracted form of se io ('if I'); son stato is the perfect form ('I have been')—literally 'am been' in older Italian—used to say the speaker has been among that people; tra quella gente = 'among that people'; che piange l’avarizia is a relative clause with piange (present, poetical/habitual) 'who bewail/mourn avarice.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g49: Per purgarmi is a purpose phrase: per + infinitive (purgarmi = 'to purge myself' / 'to cleanse myself'). The clitic -mi is attached to the infinitive and marks the reflexive/personal object.
segments.purgatorio-22-g50: Per lo contrario suo m’è incontrato is a perfect construction with the clitic object m’ + auxiliary è (from essere) + past participle incontrato: 'has met me' or 'has come upon me.' Per lo contrario suo = 'by/through its opposite' (i.e. the contrary of the thing under discussion). Word order places the prepositional phrase first for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-22-g51: Or = 'now' or 'then' as a discourse particle. Quando tu cantasti uses the passato remoto (cantasti, 2nd person singular) 'when you sang' (referring to a previously recounted song/text). Le crude armi is a poetic expression ('the cruel arms' = the cruel deeds/strife) and de la doppia trestizia di Giocasta is a genitive construction (de la = archaic di + la) 'of the double sorrow/misery of Jocasta.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g52: Disse is the simple past ('he said'); ’l cantor is the subject 'the singer' (the apostrophe marks the elided article). De’ buccolici carmi = 'of bucolic (pastoral) songs/poems.' Word order is the normal reporter clause following the quoted line.
segments.purgatorio-22-g53: Per quello che = 'because' / 'for the reason that.' Clïò teco lì tasta: Clio (the Muse of history) + teco ('with you') + lì ('there') + tasta ('tests/tries' — present indicative). Tasta here means 'tests' or 'puts to the test' rather than a literal 'taste.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g54: Non par che introduces a clause of appearance ('it does not seem that...') and requires the subjunctive. Facesse is the imperfect subjunctive of fare used here as 'made' (la fede facesse ti ancor fedele = 'that faith had as yet made you faithful'). Ti is the clitic object. La fede (faith) is the logical subject placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-22-g55: Sanza qual = 'without which' (sanza = without; qual = 'which' referring back to la fede). Ben far is a nominalized infinitive phrase meaning 'doing good' (ben = well/good, far = fare 'to do'). Non basta = 'is not sufficient.' The whole clause asserts that without faith, doing good alone does not suffice.
segments.purgatorio-22-g56: Se così è is a simple conditional opening: se = 'if', così = 'so/thus', è = 'it is.' It frames the following question as conditional on the previous statement being true.
segments.purgatorio-22-g57: Qual sole o quai candele is a poetic interrogative 'what sun or what candles' (quai is an archaic plural form of quale). Ti is the clitic object 'you.' Stenebraron is passato remoto (3rd plural) meaning 'darkened' or 'obscured' — it agrees with the compound subject 'qual sole o quai candele.' Sì = 'so' (to that degree).
segments.purgatorio-22-g58: Che introduces a result/relative clause: 'that' / 'so that.' Tu drizzasti is passato remoto 2nd singular 'you straightened/raised' (drizzare here = to set upright/trim). Poscia = 'afterwards'; di retro al pescator le vele is a prepositional phrase with di retro 'behind' + al pescator 'to/at the fisherman' + le vele 'the sails' (object). The whole asks why/under what influence you later turned your sails to follow (or move behind) the fisherman.
segments.purgatorio-22-g59: This segment is a single punctuation mark (full stop) ending the speech/verse; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-22-g122: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed at the start to link to what came before. L’usanza is the grammatical subject, fu is the passato remoto of essere (‘was’), and lì is a locative adverb 'there' inserted before the predicate nominative nostra insegna ('our sign/standard'); the phrase literally says 'Thus the custom was there our signal' (poetic word order). The l’ is the elided definite article.
segments.purgatorio-22-g123: Prendemmo is the passato remoto, first-person plural, 'we took' or 'we set out'; la via is the direct object 'the road/the way'. Con men sospetto is an adverbial phrase 'with less suspicion' (men is the archaic poetic form of meno). Per introduces the reason ('because/through/by means of') and l’assentir is a noun (assent/consent) formed from the verb; di quell’anima degna is a genitive/agent phrase 'of that worthy soul'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g124: Elli is an archaic third-person plural pronoun 'they' (used here for the two poets). Givan is the imperfect of 'to go' (poetically written) and gives the background action 'were going/went'; dinanzi is an adverb 'in front/ahead' placed after the verb.
segments.purgatorio-22-g125: E io soletto di retro places the speaker in contrast: e io 'and I', soletto is an adjective meaning 'alone/by myself' (a poetic form of solito/solitario), and di retro is an adverbial phrase 'at the rear/behind'. The structure emphasizes that the poet stayed behind alone.
segments.purgatorio-22-g126: Ascoltava is the imperfect (habitual/past continuous) 'I listened/was listening' with the understood subject io from the previous clause; i lor sermoni is the direct object 'their speeches/discourses', where lor is the poetic possessive 'their'. The imperfect frames this as an ongoing receptive action.
segments.purgatorio-22-g127: Ch’ (= che) introduces a relative/causal clause 'which/that'. A + infinitive (a poetar) marks the activity 'to compose poetry' and functions as the complement of intelletto. Mi is the indirect object pronoun 'to me', davano is the imperfect third-person plural 'they gave', and intelletto is the noun 'understanding'—so 'their speeches gave me the understanding to compose poetry.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g128: Ma tosto 'but soon' introduces a quick interruption. Ruppe is passato remoto 'broke/interrupted' and takes le dolci ragioni ('the sweet words/speech') as its object; the grammatical agent un alber ('a tree') follows the verb (inverted word order for poetic effect). Che trovammo in mezza strada is a relative clause 'that we found in the middle of the road' modifying un alber.
segments.purgatorio-22-g129: Con pomi a odorar soavi e buoni is a descriptive phrase: con pomi 'with fruits', a + infinitive (a odorar) functions adjectivally to mean 'giving off a scent', and soavi e buoni are adjectives 'sweet/mild and pleasant/good'. Together: 'with fruits that gave off a sweet, pleasant scent.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g130: E come introduces a simile 'and as/just as'. Abete here is the noun 'fir' (tree) despite the sense list given; in alto 'on high' locates it, and si digrada is a pronominal verb 'digradare' meaning 'slopes/descends' (si is the reflexive/pronominal particle). Di ramo in ramo is a repetitive prepositional phrase 'from branch to branch' indicating successive descent.
segments.purgatorio-22-g131: Così is 'so/thus' linking the simile back to the tree; quello is a demonstrative pronoun 'that one' referring to the tree, and in giuso is an adverbial phrase 'downward/below'. The clause states that the other tree sloped downward in the same way.
segments.purgatorio-22-g132: Cred’ io is the poet's parenthetical 'I believe'. Cred' is a contracted form of credo (the final vowel dropped for the verse), and io is the emphatic subject pronoun.
segments.purgatorio-22-g133: Perché introduces the subordinate clause and here works with the subjunctive vada to express purpose or result ('so that/no one may go up'); persona is an indefinite noun 'person/anyone' (the provided senses are for a different lemma, so the applicable sense is not listed), sù 'up', non is negation, and vada is the present subjunctive of andare ('may go/go up'). The whole clause conveys a negative purpose/result—no person goes up.
segments.purgatorio-22-g134: 'Dal' = 'da + il' ('from the'); 'lato' = 'side'; 'onde' is a relative/adverbial 'from which/where'; 'il cammin nostro era chiuso' = 'our path was closed/blocked' with 'era' (imperfect 'was') + adjective/participial 'chiuso' ('closed').
segments.purgatorio-22-g135: 'cadea' is the imperfect ('was falling' / 'fell') of 'cadere' used poetically; 'de' = 'from/of'; 'l'alta roccia' = 'the high rock' (Dante often places adjective after noun). 'un liquor chiaro' = 'a clear liquid'; 'si spandeva' is pronominal 'it spread itself' (imperf.), and 'per le foglie suso' = 'through the leaves up above' ('suso' = 'up').
segments.purgatorio-22-g1: Già is a fronted adverb meaning “already.” Era is the imperfect of essere used here as a past copula (“had/was”). L’angel is the definite article + noun; dietro a noi rimaso is a past participle construction where rimaso (past participle of rimanere) follows the auxiliary and says the angel “remained behind us.” The clause literally reads “Already was the angel remained behind us.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g2: This relative clause identifies that angel. L’angel che = “the angel who…”; n’ here is the enclitic ne (contracted) referring to us as the direct object (i.e. “who had turned us”). Avea vòlti is the imperfect auxiliary avere + past participle vòlti, meaning “had turned” (imperfect tense used narratively). Al sesto giro = “to the sixth giro/circuit.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g3: Avendomi is the gerund/participle form of avere plus the clitic mi (“having to me”); it begins an absolute participial clause and here means “having (to) me/after having) …”. Dal viso modifies the following noun (‘from the face’ = “to/against my face”), un colpo raso is the object: colpo “a blow/strike” and raso (“grazing/smooth”) describes the blow — together meaning he grazed my face with a light stroke.
segments.purgatorio-22-g4: E quei begins a new clause: e = “and”; quei = “those” (the spirits). The following relative/correlative (c’) is intended as che “who/that” (here the provided lemma list doesn't include che, so marked below as unknown). Hanno a giustizia lor disiro = literally “have toward justice their desire,” i.e. “whose desire is for justice.” Detto n’avea beati is an inversion: the angel (understood subject) aveva detto (“had said”) beati to them/us — n’ (ne) is a clitic referring back (here effectively “to us”/“of it”), and beati is the word pronounced — the syntax is compressed by poetry.
segments.purgatorio-22-g5: E le sue voci = “and his voices.” Con introduces the quoted Latin syllable that follows: ‘sitiunt’ is quoted intact (Latin third-person plural “they thirst”). The phrase links the angel’s spoken word (“beati”) with the voices that then uttered the Latin cry.
segments.purgatorio-22-g6: Sanz’ altro is a compressed, poetic form of senza altro: sanz (senza) = “without,” altro = “anything else.” It qualifies the voices’ utterance: the voices said only that word, without anything more.
segments.purgatorio-22-g7: Ciò = “that/this” (referring back to the prior content). Forniro is an older/poetic form of fornirono (passato remoto, 3rd plural) “they furnished/supplied”; the plural subject is le sue voci. So: “his voices, with ‘sitiunt’ and nothing else, supplied that.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g8: E io più lieve = “and I (was) lighter/more lightly (moving).” Che after a comparative means “than”: più ... che = “more ... than.” Per l’altre foci = “through/by the other mouths/entrances.” M’andava is the imperfect andare with clitic mi (poetic clitic placement) meaning “I was going,” describing the manner of motion.
segments.purgatorio-22-g9: Sì che introduces a result clause “so that.” Sanz’ alcun labore = senza alcun lavoro, “without any labor/effort.” Seguiva in sù li spiriti veloci = the swift spirits followed upward; the verb appears before the noun (poetic inversion), with seguiva (imperfect) indicating habitual or continuous past action.
segments.purgatorio-22-g10: Quando = “when,” a temporal conjunction. Virgilio is the subject name. Incominciò is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of incominciare: “began.” It introduces Virgil’s ensuing speech.
segments.purgatorio-22-g11: Amore is the vocative/nominative address: Virgil begins speaking to the personified Love. In Italian poetry nouns can be used directly as forms of address.
segments.purgatorio-22-g12: Acceso is a past participle/adjectival use of accendere meaning “kindled/ignited.” Di virtù indicates cause or quality ('by/with virtue'), so “kindled by virtue.” Virtù here = moral excellence/power (the poetic sense “virtue”).
segments.purgatorio-22-g13: Sempre altro accese: the verb accese (past tense of accendere) agrees with the subject Love and means “(it) kindled/ignited.” Sempre = “always”; altro = “another/other.” The clause means “it always kindled another [flame].”
segments.purgatorio-22-g14: Pur che = a concessive/conditional conjunction meaning “provided that/so long as.” La fiamma sua = “his/its flame.” Paresse is the imperfect subjunctive of parere (“might seem/appear” in older usage), and fore is an archaic/adverbial form meaning “outside/outward.” Together: “provided that his flame might appear outwardly.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g15: Onde da l’ora che = “whence/from the time when.” Da l’ora che introduces a temporal clause. Tra noi discese = “descended among us” (discese = passato remoto of discendere). Nel limbo de lo ’nferno Giovenale places Giovenale (Juvenal) as the subject who descended into the Limbo/edge of Hell; word order is poetic, placing the proper name after the locative phrase.
segments.purgatorio-22-g16: Che = “which/that” introducing a relative clause. La tua affezion = “your affection/attachment.” Mi fé palese = fé (archaic fece) “made” and palese “plain/manifest”; mi is the clitic of reference. The structure means “which your affection made plain to me.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g17: Mia benvoglienza inverso te fu quale = “my goodwill toward you was such that …” Inverso te = “toward you.” Più strinse mai = “had ever bound/tightened more” (strinse = past of stringere, used metaphorically). Di non vista persona = “than to a person not seen” (non vista = unseen). The whole clause compares his goodwill toward the addressee with that toward any unseen person.
segments.purgatorio-22-g18: Sì ch’or = “so that now.” Mi parran is the verb parere with clitic mi and old 3rd pl. form parran (they appear to me). Corte = “short” (adjective) modifies queste scale = “these stairs.” The result clause: “so that these stairs now seem short to me.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g19: Ma = “but.” Dimmi is the imperative (tell me) or familiar command with clitic mi attached: di’ + mi → dimmi.
segments.purgatorio-22-g20: E come amico mi perdona = “and as a friend forgive me” (come amico = “in the capacity of/like a friend,” perdona is present imperative/indicative ‘forgive’ with clitic mi). Se troppa sicurtà m’allarga il freno = conditional clause “if too much confidence widens/loosens my rein”: se = if, troppa sicurtà = excessive assurance/confidence, m’allarga = allarga with enclitic mi, il freno = “the rein/restraint.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g21: E come amico omai meco ragiona = “and as a friend, now converse/reason with me.” Omai = by now/now, meco = archaic for con me (“with me”), ragiona is an imperative or exhortative present (“talk/reason”).
segments.purgatorio-22-g22: Come poté trovar = “how could (it) find” (poté = passato remoto of potere, 3rd sg. “could”), trovar is the infinitive that follows. Dentro al tuo seno = “within your breast/bosom” (seno = metaphorical heart/inner self). Loco avarizia = “a place (for) avarice” (loco is the noun ‘place’ and avarizia is the vice).
segments.purgatorio-22-g23: Tra cotanto senno = “among so much wisdom/sense” (tra = among; cotanto = so great). Di quanto per tua cura fosti pieno = “of how much by your care you were full” — di quanto introduces the measure, per tua cura = “through/by your care,” fosti (passato remoto of essere) = “you were,” pieno = “full.” The clause questions how greed could exist given the addressee’s wisdom.
segments.purgatorio-22-g24: This segment is just the final punctuation mark ending the passage.
segments.purgatorio-22-g78: Vennermi is an archaic past form of vennero with the enclitic pronoun mi attached (vennero + mi → vennermi), meaning “they came to me.” Parendo is the present participle of parere, here functioning adverbially: “appearing.” Poi is a simple adverb “then/afterwards.” Tanto intensifies santi: “so/so very.” The whole phrase = “they came to me, appearing so holy.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g79: Che introduces the subordinate clause that follows (equivalent to English “that”), linking the appearance just described to the consequence that follows.
segments.purgatorio-22-g80: Quando is the temporal conjunction “when.” Domizian (Domitian) is the proper name in the subject position (archaic spelling). Li is the unstressed object pronoun (them) cliticized before the verb; here it marks the object of perseguette. Perseguette is the passato remoto (third person singular) of perseguire used transitively: “he persecuted.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g81: Sanza = senza, “without.” Mio is a possessive “my,” here modifying lagrimar (the infinitive used as a noun: “my weeping/tears”). Non + fur is an archaic past copula (fur = furono/erano) giving “were not.” Lor is the plural possessive/oblique “their” (their). Pianti here is poetic/plural form of pianto = “laments/tears.” The clause means “their lamentations were not without my weeping.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g82: E = “and.” Mentre che = “while/whereas”; che often follows mentre in older usage. Di là = “there” / “on that side”; per me = “because of me” or “on my account.” Si is the reflexive/impersonal clitic used with stette; stette is passato remoto (third sing.) of stare/stare fermo meaning “remained/was in place.” The segment: “and while they stood there on my account.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g83: Io is the explicit subject “I.” Li is the object pronoun “them.” Sovvenni is the first person singular passato remoto of sovvenire here in the active sense “I helped/relieved/assisted.” So: “I helped them.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g84: E = “and.” I + lor = the plural article + their (i lor) marks “their” as a possessive phrase. Dritti is an adjective here meaning “upright/right” (moral sense). Costumi = “customs/ways/behaviour.” Fer is an archaic (poetic) past form meaning “they made/caused” (from fare/fecer). Dispregiare is the infinitive “to despise/slight”; a modifies the indirect object (a me = towards me / for me). Tutte altre sette = “all other sects” (sette = sects/groups). The construction fer dispregiare a me tutte altre sette = “caused all other sects to be despised by me” or “made me despise all other sects.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g85: E = “and.” Pria = “before.” Ch’ is the clitic form of che introducing a subordinate clause. Io is the subject pronoun “I.” Conducessi is an imperfect/subjunctive-like past form meaning “I led” (poetic/archaic), here aoristic narration: “before I led.” I Greci = “the Greeks.” A’ fiumi di Tebe = “to the rivers of Thebes” (di Tebe = of Thebes). Poetando is a gerund/participle “poetizing/while composing poetry,” specifying the manner or context.
segments.purgatorio-22-g86: Ebb’ (contracted from ebbi/ebbe) is an archaic past form of avere used here to mean “I had” (possession/experience). Io is the subject. Battesmo is the noun “baptism.” The clause states a past possession/condition: “I had baptism” (i.e. I was baptized).
segments.purgatorio-22-g87: Ma = “but.” Per paura = “for/because of fear.” Chiuso is an adjective meaning “closed/secret/enclosed”; cristian = “Christian (person).” Fu’ mi is an archaic copular construction (fu’ = fu, “was”) with the clitic mi attached to indicate the first person: “I was” (literally “was to me”). The line means “but out of fear I was a concealed/secret Christian.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g88: Lungamente = “for a long time.” Mostrando is the present participle/gerund of mostrare, “showing/displaying.” Paganesmo is the noun “paganism” or “pagan outward behaviour/classical culture.” Together: “long showing paganism” = “long presenting/maintaining pagan behaviour.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g89: E = “and.” Questa = “this.” Tepidezza = “lukewarmness” (figurative spiritual tepidness). Il quarto cerchio = “the fourth circle” (circle/ring). Cerchiar is the infinitive “to encircle/ encompass”; mi is the clitic object “me.” Fé (fè) is the past tense of fare (fece) used impersonally to mean “caused/made.” Più che = “more than.” ’L quarto centesmo = “the fourth hundredth” (literal ordinal). The construction means that this tepidity caused the fourth circle to enclose him to a greater degree than some other measure (a comparative/poetic hyperbole).
segments.purgatorio-22-g90: Tu is the vocative/subject “you.” Dunque is a discourse adverb “then/therefore,” addressing the addressee and introducing a request based on what was just said.
segments.purgatorio-22-g91: Che introduces a relative clause: “who/which.” Levato is the past participle of levare, here used with the auxiliary hai to mean “have lifted/removed.” Hai is the present of avere (you have). Il coperchio = “the lid/cover.” Che (second occurrence) again introduces the relative clause modifying coperchio. M’ is the enclitic me (to me). Ascondeva is the imperfect of nascondere (ascondere in older spelling) meaning “it hid/was hiding.” Quanto bene io dico = literal “how well I say” or “how well I speak” (quanto bene = “how much good/ how well”). So: “you who have lifted the lid that hid from me how well I speak.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g92: Mentre che = “while.” Del salire = “of the ascent” (del = di + il). Avem is the archaic first-person plural present of avere (we have). Soverchio = “surplus/excess/abundance.” The phrase means “while we have an abundance of ascent” i.e. while there is plenty of climb/time left.
segments.purgatorio-22-g93: Dimmi = imperative “tell me.” Dov’ is the contracted dove (“where”). È is third singular present of essere, “is” (located). Terrenzio = the proper name Terence (Terrence). Nostro = our. Antico = “ancient” or here “our ancient/foremost” (referring to a classical author). The clause: “Tell me where our ancient Terence is.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g94: A simple enumeration of proper names: Cecilio, Plauto and Varro — the Romans Caecilius, Plautus and Varro — connected by coordinating e = “and.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g95: Se lo sai — se = “if/whether” (introducing a conditional question), lo = neutral object pronoun “it” (referring to the information), sai = second-person singular present of sapere “you know.” Together: “if you know it.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g96: Dimmi = imperative “tell me.” Se = “whether/if.” Son is the contracted form of sono (they are) used for plural third person. Dannati is the past participle/adjective “damned” (condemned). The question: “Tell me whether they are damned.”
segments.purgatorio-22-g97: E = “and.” In = “in/at.” Qual = “which/what” used interrogatively. Vico = “lane/street” (here: which lane/street of Hell/Purgatory). The final request: “and in which lane?”
segments.purgatorio-22-g98: 'Costoro' is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'these/those people' and begins the enumerative list; Dante strings the items with repeated 'e' (and) for parataxis. 'assai' follows the plural 'altri' to mean 'many'/'numerous' (postpositive adverb).
segments.purgatorio-22-g99: 'rispuose' is the passato remoto (past historic) form of 'rispondere' meaning 'he answered.' 'il duca mio' places the possessive after the noun (typical Italian word order) and here 'duca' functions as 'guide' (Virgil), not a secular duke.
segments.purgatorio-22-g100: 'siam' is an archaic/poetic form corresponding to modern 'siamo' ('we are'); it takes the preposition 'con' to mark accompaniment. The relative 'che' introduces the clause describing 'quel Greco'; 'lattar' is used figuratively (= 'to suckle/feed on milk'), so 'che le Muse lattar più ch'altri mai' = 'who more than any others suckled the Muses.' The elided 'ch’' in the comparative is the shortened 'che' used as 'than'.
segments.purgatorio-22-g101: 'nel' = 'in the' (in + il). 'primo' is ordinal 'first'; 'cinghio' here is a noun meaning 'girdle' or 'belt' (a ring/encircling), poetically applied to the circle of Purgatory. 'del carcere cieco' = 'of the blind prison'—'cieco' qualifies 'carcere.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g102: 'spesse fïate' is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'often, oftentimes' (literally 'frequent breaths/times'); 'ragioniam' is an archaic present-tense form corresponding to modern 'ragioniamo' ('we reason/discuss'). 'del monte' is the object of discussion; the relative 'che' introduces the clause 'which always has our nurses with it' — 'seco' = 'with it/him.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g136: Li is the archaic masculine plural definite article (modern Italian i/ i/ gli is different in position), followed by the noun phrase due poeti. a l’alber = a + l’ (to the) with alber an archaic form of albero (tree). s’ + appressaro is the pronominal verb s'appressar (appressare) in passato remoto, 3rd person plural: 'they drew near' (the s’ is the reflexive pronoun si marking 'to draw near'). Word order places the prepositional phrase after the subject.
segments.purgatorio-22-g137: e is the simple conjunction 'and'. una voce is 'a voice' (una = indefinite article). per entro is a doubled prepositional phrase meaning 'through/within' (emphatic 'from inside'), le fronde = 'the foliage/leafy branches'. gridò is passato remoto, 3rd singular of gridare: 'cried out'. The whole phrase describes a voice issuing from inside the leaves.
segments.purgatorio-22-g138: Di questo cibo is a prepositional phrase marking the thing affected ('this food'); avrete is future indicative, 2nd person plural of avere ('you will have'), used idiomatically with an adjective to mean 'you will regard/consider'; caro is an adjective meaning 'dear/precious' so the clause means 'you will hold this food dear' or 'you will value this food.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g139: Poi = 'then/afterwards'. disse is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of dire: 'he said.' This introduces reported speech (the quotation that follows).
segments.purgatorio-22-g140: Più pensava Maria is a comparative construction: 'Mary thought more (i.e. gave more thought)'. Più ... che (completed later) sets up 'more ... than ...'. onde here functions as 'whence/from where' (not the sense 'wave'); fosser is the archaic subjunctive/imperf. form of essere (here indicating indirect question or reported thought: 'had been/come from'); le nozze orrevoli e intere = 'the nuptials (weddings/marriage) described as orrevoli (horrible/odious) and intere (whole/intact).' Note: word choices are archaic/poetic.
segments.purgatorio-22-g141: ch’ a la sua bocca: the ch’ is the clitic form of che used in comparative '... che ...' here functioning as 'than' (classical/comparative use, not one of the ordinary senses of che). a la sua bocca = 'to her own mouth' or 'about her own lips/speech' — i.e. 'more than about her own mouth.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g142: ch’ here is a relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing the relative clause; per voi = 'for you/on your behalf' (per marking beneficiary); risponde is present indicative of rispondere: 'answers' or 'responds' (the mouth 'answers' now on behalf of you). The sequence ch’or (ch’ + or) in the text means 'which now' (or = ora, 'now').
segments.purgatorio-22-g143: E = 'and'. le Romane antiche = 'the ancient Romans' (Romane here an adjective; in Dante the feminine plural can refer to Roman women or to Romans of the olden time, and antiche = 'ancient'). The phrase sets the subject for the clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-22-g144: per lor bere: per here means 'with respect to' or 'as regards'; lor is the archaic clitic for 'loro/their'; bere is the infinitive 'to drink' used substantively: 'as to their drinking' or 'for what concerns their drinking.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g145: contente furon = contente (feminine plural) + furon (passato remoto, 3rd plural of essere) gives 'they were content/satisfied.' d’acqua = di + acqua, here meaning 'with water' or 'of water' — i.e. they were satisfied by water.
segments.purgatorio-22-g146: e = 'and'. Danïello is a proper name. dispregiò is passato remoto, 3rd singular of dispregiare: 'he despised/scorned.' cibo = 'food.' e acquistò savere: acquistò (passato remoto of acquistare) = 'acquired' and savere (archaic spelling of sapere used as noun) = 'knowledge' or 'learning' — together 'acquired knowledge' or 'gained wisdom.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g147: Lo is the masculine singular definite article 'the' used before secol (secol is an archaic form of secolo = 'age/era' here); primo = 'first' (the first age/era). The phrase establishes the temporal frame.
segments.purgatorio-22-g148: quant’ is a contracted form of quanto meaning 'as much as/when'. oro = 'gold'; fu is passato remoto, 3rd singular of essere 'was'; bello = 'beautiful/fair' — together 'when/while gold was fair' (i.e. in an age when gold was valued/beautiful).
segments.purgatorio-22-g149: fé is the archaic passato remoto he/she/it form of fare (fece) 'made/caused'. savorose = 'savoury/tasty' (agrees in gender/number with ghiande); con fame is 'by/through hunger' (here fame = 'hunger' in the poet's usage — note the lemma given elsewhere may list different senses, so this is a semantic usage); le ghiande = 'the acorns' (direct object). The verb thus means 'hunger made the acorns seem tasty.'
segments.purgatorio-22-g150: e = 'and'. nettare = 'nectar' (used metaphorically). con sete = 'with thirst' (i.e. through thirst); ogne (archaic ogn + e) = 'every'; ruscello = 'brook/stream'. The inversion places the effect (nectar) before the cause ('with thirst every stream') and means 'thirst made every stream taste like nectar.'

sentences.purgatorio-22-s17: And he to him: "You first sent me toward Parnassus to drink in its caves, and first thereafter you enlightened me toward God."
sentences.purgatorio-22-s18: You acted like one who goes by night, who carries the light behind him and it does him no good, but after himself makes people learned, when you said: 'The age renews itself; justice returns and the first human time, and a new progeny descends from heaven.'"
sentences.purgatorio-22-s19: It was through you that I became a poet, through you a Christian; but so that you may better see what I intend, I will stretch out my hand to add color.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s20: By then the whole world was already thoroughly full of the true faith, sown by the messengers of the eternal kingdom; and your word, touched on that, harmonized with the new preachers; therefore I took up the custom of visiting them.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s9: And if it had not been that I turned my attention, when I heard there, where you call, almost vexed at human nature: 'Why do you not restrain, O sacred hunger for gold, the appetite of mortals?'
sentences.purgatorio-22-s10: ', turning, I would hear the doleful jousts.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s11: Then I realized that the hands could open their wings too widely to spend, and I repented thus of that as I did of the other evils.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s12: How many will rise again with cropped hair through ignorance, who by this sin lose repentance while living and even in their last moments!
sentences.purgatorio-22-s37: Apples and locusts were the foods that nourished the Baptist in the desert; therefore he is glorious and so great as is revealed in the Gospel.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s25: Euripides is here with us, and Antiphon, Simonides, Agathon and many other Greeks who once adorned their brows with laurel.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s26: There one sees among your people Antigone, Deïfile and Argia, and Ismene as sorrowful as she was.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s27: One sees there the woman who showed Langia; there is the daughter of Tiresias, and Thetis, and Deïdamia with her sisters.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s28: Both poets were already silent, once more attentive to look about, free from climbs and from rock faces; and already the four handmaids of the day had remained behind, and the fifth was at the verge, still raising the burning horn upward, when my guide said: "I think that at the extremity it is fitting to turn our right shoulders, circling the mountain as we were wont to do."
sentences.purgatorio-22-s5: These words at first moved Statius to a little smile; then he answered: "Every word you say of love is a dear sign to me."
sentences.purgatorio-22-s6: Truly, things often appear that give cause to doubt a matter is false, because the true reasons are hidden.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s7: Your question — your believing — confirms to me that I was miserly in my other life, perhaps because of that circle in which I was.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s8: Know now that avarice was too far removed from me, and this excess has been punished by thousands of lunar months.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s13: And know that the fault which, by direct opposition, reproves any sin, together with it here withers its green; therefore, if I have been among that people who bewail avarice in order to purge myself, its very opposite has met me.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s14: "Now when you sang the cruel arms of Jocasta's double sorrow," said the singer of bucolic verse, "because Clio there tests you with it, it does not seem that faith had yet made you faithful — faith, without which doing good is not sufficient."
sentences.purgatorio-22-s15: If that is so, which sun or which candles darkened you so, that afterwards you straightened the sails behind the fisher?
sentences.purgatorio-22-s29: Thus our custom served as our signal there, and we took the road with less suspicion because of the assent of that worthy soul.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s30: They went ahead, and I, by myself at the rear, listened to their speeches, which gave me the understanding to compose poetry.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s31: But soon the sweet talk was broken off by a tree that we found in the middle of the road, bearing fruits that gave off a sweet and pleasant scent; and as a fir on high slopes down from branch to branch, so that one sloped downward, I believe, so that no person may go up.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s32: From the side where our path was closed, a clear liquid fell from the high rock and spread itself through the leaves above.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s1: The angel who had turned us to the sixth circle had already stayed behind us, having brushed my face with a light stroke; and to those whose desire was for justice he had said “beati,” and his voices, with “sitiunt” and nothing else, supplied that.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s2: And I went more lightly than by the other mouths, so that, without any toil, the swift spirits followed upward; when Virgil began: “Love, kindled by virtue, ever kindled another, provided that his flame seemed outward; and from the hour when Juvenal descended among us into the Limbo of Hell — which your attachment made plain to me — my goodwill toward you was such that it had ever bound me more than toward a person not seen, so that now these stairs seem short to me.”
sentences.purgatorio-22-s3: But tell me, and as a friend forgive me if excessive assurance loosens my rein, and now speak with me as a friend: how could avarice find a place within your breast among so much wisdom with which, by your care, you were filled?
sentences.purgatorio-22-s21: They then came to me appearing so holy that, when Domitian persecuted them, their lamentations were not without my tears; and while they remained there on my account I helped them, and their upright conduct made me despise all other sects.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s22: And before I led the Greeks to the rivers of Thebes in my verse, I had been baptized; but through fear I was a secret Christian, for a long time showing paganism; and this lukewarmness made the fourth circle encompass me more than the four-hundredth.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s23: You then, who have lifted the lid that hid from me how well I speak, while we have an abundance of ascent, tell me where our ancient Terence is, Caecilius and Plautus and Varro, if you know it; tell me whether they are damned, and in which lane.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s24: "These men and Persius and I and many others," answered my guide, "are with that Greek who suckled the Muses more than any other ever did, in the first girdle of the blind prison; oftentimes we reason about the mountain which always has our nurses with it."
sentences.purgatorio-22-s33: The two poets drew near to the tree; and a voice cried out from among the leaves: "You will hold this food dear."
sentences.purgatorio-22-s34: Then he said: "Mary thought more about whence the dread and whole nuptials came than about her own mouth, which now answers for you."
sentences.purgatorio-22-s35: And the ancient Romans, as to their drinking, were content with water; and Daniello scorned food and gained knowledge.
sentences.purgatorio-22-s36: The first age — when gold was as beautiful as it could be — made acorns tasty through hunger, and every brook became nectar through thirst.
